name: esm
context: ES Modules in Node.js: import/export syntax, package.json type field, CommonJS interop, conditional exports, and dual-package publishing
info: ES Modules (ESM) are the official JavaScript module standard, and Node.js has supported them natively since v12 (unflagged since v13.2). ESM replaces CommonJS (CJS) as the forward-looking module system, bringing static analysis, tree-shaking, top-level await, and alignment with browser JavaScript. The transition from CJS to ESM is one of the most consequential shifts in the Node.js ecosystem, affecting how packages are authored, published, and consumed. Enabling ESM requires setting `"type": "module"` in package.json or using the `.mjs` extension. The `import.meta` object provides ESM-specific metadata like `import.meta.url`, `import.meta.dirname` (v21+), and `import.meta.filename`. The package.json `"exports"` field enables conditional exports for dual CJS/ESM packages, subpath exports, and environment-specific entry points. Understanding ESM is essential for modern Node.js development and for building packages that work across the ecosystem.
triggers: set up es modules in node
create a dual package for npm
enable es modules in node project
use top level await in node

sections.title: Enabling ESM in Node.js
sections.body: Set `"type": "module"` in package.json to treat all `.js` files as ESM, or use the `.mjs` extension for individual files. Files with `.cjs` are always CommonJS. Without a `"type"` field, `.js` defaults to CommonJS. The `--input-type=module` flag can force ESM for `--eval` and STDIN input. When `"type": "module"` is set, the nearest parent package.json determines the mode for all `.js` files in that directory tree.

sections.title: import and export syntax
sections.body: ESM provides static `import` and `export` declarations that are analyzed before execution. Named exports (`export function foo()`) allow selective importing (`import { foo }`). Default exports (`export default`) provide a single primary export. Namespace imports (`import * as mod`) gather all exports. Re-exports (`export { x } from './mod.js'`) create barrel files. Unlike CJS `require()`, ESM imports are live bindings: if the exporting module changes a value, the importing module sees the update. File extensions are mandatory for relative imports in Node.js ESM.

sections.title: import.meta properties
sections.body: `import.meta` is an object available only in ESM that provides module-level metadata. `import.meta.url` returns the `file://` URL of the current module. Node.js v21.2+ adds `import.meta.dirname` and `import.meta.filename` as direct equivalents of CJS `__dirname`/`__filename`. `import.meta.resolve(specifier)` resolves a module specifier to a URL without loading the module (synchronous in Node.js v20.6+). These properties are essential for path resolution in ESM, replacing the CJS globals that are not available in ES modules.

sections.title: CommonJS interop
sections.body: ESM can import CJS modules: `import pkg from './cjs-module.cjs'` imports the `module.exports` as the default export. Named imports from CJS may work if Node.js can statically analyze the exports, but this is not guaranteed. CJS CANNOT `require()` ESM — it must use dynamic `import()`. This asymmetry is the biggest pain point in the CJS-to-ESM transition. For packages that must support both worlds, use conditional exports in package.json. The `createRequire()` function from `module` lets ESM files use `require()` for CJS-only packages that resist dynamic import.

sections.title: Package exports and conditional exports
sections.body: The `"exports"` field in package.json defines the public entry points of a package and encapsulates internal files. Subpath exports map import specifiers to file paths. Conditional exports serve different files based on context: `"import"` for ESM, `"require"` for CJS, `"node"` for Node.js, `"browser"` for browser bundlers, `"types"` for TypeScript declarations, and `"default"` as a fallback. Conditions are evaluated in order — put more specific conditions first. Subpath patterns (`"./*.js"`) allow wildcard exports. Setting an export to `null` blocks access. The `"exports"` field takes precedence over `"main"` when present.

sections.title: Dual-package publishing
sections.body: Publishing a package that works with both `require()` and `import` uses conditional exports to point to CJS and ESM builds respectively. The recommended approach is to write source in ESM (or TypeScript) and build to both formats. Use `"import"` and `"require"` conditions in the `"exports"` map. Beware the dual-package hazard: if the same package is loaded via both module systems in one application, two separate copies exist, breaking singletons and `instanceof`. Mitigate by isolating state or using an ESM wrapper over a CJS core. See [[node/npm/publish]] for publishing workflows.

sections.title: Top-level await
sections.body: ESM supports `await` at the top level of a module, outside any async function. This enables async initialization (database connections, config fetching, dynamic imports) at module load time. When a module uses top-level await, all modules that import it will wait for the await to resolve before executing. This can delay application startup, so use it judiciously in library code. Top-level await is not available in CommonJS.

sections.title: JSON imports
sections.body: ESM can import JSON files using import attributes (the `with` keyword). Syntax: `import data from './data.json' with { type: 'json' }`. The older `assert` keyword is deprecated but still works in Node.js 18-20.9. JSON is imported as the default export (no named exports). Dynamic import of JSON: `const data = await import('./data.json', { with: { type: 'json' } })`. As an alternative, read and parse JSON manually using `fs.readFile` and `JSON.parse`.
